title: Cherry Valley massacre
supporting_sentences: Historian Barbara Graymont describes Butler's command of the expedition as "criminally incompetent".

The Seneca were angered by accusations that they had committed atrocities at the Battle of Wyoming, and the colonists' recent destruction of their forward bases of operation at Unadilla, Onaquaga, and Tioga.
Butler's authority with the Indians was undermined by his poor treatment of Joseph Brant, the leader of the Mohawks.

Diaries belonging to British soldiers during the campaign state the regiment as being the "butchers" and given that Butler was the overall commander of the expedition, there is controversy as to who actually ordered or failed to restrain the killings.
The massacre contributed to calls for reprisals, leading to the 1779 Sullivan Expedition which drove the Iroquois out of western New York.
gold_answer_phrase: drove the Iroquois out of western New York
gold_answer_sentence: The massacre contributed to calls for reprisals, leading to the 1779 Sullivan Expedition which drove the Iroquois out of western New York.

title: Cherry Valley massacre
supporting_sentences: Historian Barbara Graymont describes Butler's command of the expedition as "criminally incompetent".

The Seneca were angered by accusations that they had committed atrocities at the Battle of Wyoming, and the colonists' recent destruction of their forward bases of operation at Unadilla, Onaquaga, and Tioga.
Butler's authority with the Indians was undermined by his poor treatment of Joseph Brant, the leader of the Mohawks.
Butler repeatedly maintained, against accusations that he permitted the atrocities to take place, that he was powerless to restrain the Seneca.
During the campaigns of 1778, Brant achieved an undeserved reputation for brutality.
He was not present at Wyoming — although many thought he was — and he actively sought to minimize the atrocities that took place at Cherry Valley.
gold_answer_phrase: During the campaigns of 1778, Brant achieved an undeserved reputation for brutality
gold_answer_sentence: During the campaigns of 1778, Brant achieved an undeserved reputation for brutality.

title: Cherry Valley massacre
supporting_sentences: It has been described as one of the most horrific frontier massacres of the war.
A mixed force of Loyalists, British soldiers, Seneca and Mohawks descended on Cherry Valley, whose defenders, despite warnings, were unprepared for the attack.
During the raid, the Seneca in particular targeted non-combatants, and reports state that 30 such individuals were slain, in addition to a number of armed defenders.
Butler's authority with the Indians was undermined by his poor treatment of Joseph Brant, the leader of the Mohawks.
Butler repeatedly maintained, against accusations that he permitted the atrocities to take place, that he was powerless to restrain the Seneca.
During the campaigns of 1778, Brant achieved an undeserved reputation for brutality.
He was not present at Wyoming — although many thought he was — and he actively sought to minimize the atrocities that took place at Cherry Valley.

Diaries belonging to British soldiers during the campaign state the regiment as being the "butchers" and given that Butler was the overall commander of the expedition, there is controversy as to who actually ordered or failed to restrain the killings.
The massacre contributed to calls for reprisals, leading to the 1779 Sullivan Expedition which drove the Iroquois out of western New York.
gold_answer_phrase: drove the Iroquois out of western New York
gold_answer_sentence: The massacre contributed to calls for reprisals, leading to the 1779 Sullivan Expedition which drove the Iroquois out of western New York.

title: United States occupation of Nicaragua
supporting_sentences: Nicaragua assumed a quasi-protectorate status under the 1916 Bryan–Chamorro Treaty.
President Herbert Hoover (1929–1933) opposed the relationship.
Finally in 1933 President Franklin D Roosevelt, invoking his new Good Neighbor policy ended American intervention.
gold_answer_phrase: Finally in 1933 President Franklin D Roosevelt, invoking his new Good Neighbor policy ended American intervention
gold_answer_sentence: Finally in 1933 President Franklin D Roosevelt, invoking his new Good Neighbor policy ended American intervention.

title: United States occupation of Nicaragua
supporting_sentences: The United States occupation of Nicaragua from 1912 to 1933 was part of the Banana Wars, when the US military intervened in various Latin American countries from 1898 to 1934.
The formal occupation began in 1912, even though there were various other assaults by the U.S. in Nicaragua throughout this period.
American military interventions in Nicaragua were designed to stop any other nation except the United States of America from building a Nicaraguan Canal.
Nicaragua assumed a quasi-protectorate status under the 1916 Bryan–Chamorro Treaty.
President Herbert Hoover (1929–1933) opposed the relationship.
Finally in 1933 President Franklin D Roosevelt, invoking his new Good Neighbor policy ended American intervention.
gold_answer_phrase: President Herbert Hoover (1929–1933) opposed the relationship
gold_answer_sentence: President Herbert Hoover (1929–1933) opposed the relationship.
